content: Ahimsa means non-harm to all living beings. Gandhi elevated it from a personal virtue to a political weapon. He believed that nonviolence requires more courage than violence — it demands willingness to suffer without retaliation. He said: 'An eye for an eye only ends up making the whole world blind.'

Satyagraha means 'truth-force' or 'soul-force'. It is Gandhi's method of nonviolent resistance. Unlike passive resistance, satyagraha actively confronts injustice through civil disobedience, non-cooperation, and self-suffering. The practitioner accepts punishment willingly to expose the moral wrongness of unjust laws.

On March 12, 1930, Gandhi set out from Sabarmati Ashram with 78 followers on a 240-mile march to the coastal village of Dandi. The British had imposed a tax on salt and forbidden Indians from making their own. After 24 days of walking, Gandhi picked up a handful of natural salt from the shore, symbolically breaking British law. This act of defiance inspired millions across India.

Gandhi led three major movements: Non-Cooperation (1920-22), Civil Disobedience (1930-34), and Quit India (1942). He organized millions of ordinary Indians — farmers, weavers, teachers, students — to peacefully refuse cooperation with British rule. India gained independence on August 15, 1947, after nearly 200 years of British colonial rule.

On August 8, 1942, Gandhi launched the Quit India Movement with the call 'Do or Die'. He demanded an end to British rule in India immediately. The British responded by arresting Gandhi and Congress leaders. Despite the crackdown, the movement showed that Indian self-rule was inevitable and hastened the end of colonial governance.

Gandhi was born on October 2, 1869, in Porbandar, Gujarat. He studied law in London and practiced in South Africa for 21 years (1893-1914). In South Africa, he first experienced racial discrimination and developed his methods of nonviolent resistance against the discriminatory laws affecting the Indian community there.

Gandhi believed education should develop both character and intellect. He advocated for 'Nai Talim' (basic education) — learning through productive work and community service. He said: 'Live as if you were to die tomorrow. Learn as if you were to live forever.' He believed children are the living messages we send to a time we will not see.

The charkha (spinning wheel) became a symbol of Indian self-reliance. Gandhi encouraged Indians to spin their own cloth (khadi) instead of buying British-manufactured textiles. This was both an economic boycott and a statement of dignity — that Indians could provide for themselves without depending on colonial industry.

Gandhi worked tirelessly for Hindu-Muslim unity. He undertook fasts unto death to stop communal violence. During the partition riots of 1947, while Delhi and Punjab burned with sectarian violence, Gandhi walked barefoot through riot-torn villages in Bengal, personally appealing for peace. His efforts in Calcutta were so effective that Lord Mountbatten called him a 'one-man boundary force.'

Gandhi's methods of nonviolent resistance influenced civil rights movements worldwide. Martin Luther King Jr. adopted Gandhian principles in the American civil rights movement. Nelson Mandela credited Gandhi's influence in South Africa's anti-apartheid struggle. The United Nations declared October 2 (Gandhi's birthday) as the International Day of Non-Violence.
